headline: Saving Soil, Securing Farms
fullText:
PIB Backgrounder

Saving Soil, Securing Farms

“Beneath Every Harvest Lies Healthy Soil”

24 JUL 2026 11:50AM by PIB Delhi

Soil is a strategic national resource that supports agricultural productivity, food security, and rural livelihoods in India. National initiatives are promoting scientific soil management through soil testing, balanced nutrient use, and climate-resilient farming practices. The Soil Health Card Scheme has generated over 25.89 crore cards, supporting informed nutrient management. The National Mission on Natural Farming has expanded to 18,786 clusters covering 8.80 lakh hectares. Public outreach efforts, including the Khet Bachao Abhiyan, are encouraging wider adoption of sustainable practices. Digital platforms, geospatial mapping and artificial intelligence are improving soil assessment and advisory services, strengthening productivity, resilience and long-term agricultural sustainability.

Soil as a Strategic National Resource

Soil forms the very foundation of India’s agricultural systems, supporting crop production, sustaining ecosystems, and securing livelihoods. More than 46 percent of the workforce is employed in agriculture and allied activities. As a result, soil health has a direct influence on rural incomes, food systems, and overall economic stability.

According to the Land Use Statistics 2022-23, the total reported area of India is 306.65 million hectares, of which nearly 59 per cent is under agriculture. This highlights the critical role of soil in sustaining productivity and meeting the nutritional needs of a growing population. Healthy soil enables higher yields and reduces dependence on external inputs. In this context, soil security and sustainability are vital for achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) 2030. This is particularly important for those related to zero hunger (SDG 2) and climate action (SDG 13).

Recognising this strategic importance, India has expanded its focus on sustainable soil and land management. National efforts span climate resilience, natural farming, soil mapping, and digital agriculture. Initiatives such as the National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture (NMSA) and the National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF) promote resilient and resource-efficient agricultural practices. The Soil Health Card Scheme further supports informed nutrient application through soil testing and advisory services. These efforts reflect the journey of soil science evolving from traditional farming practices to modern scientific approaches.

Alongside institutional interventions, recent awareness initiatives such as the “Khet Bachao Abhiyan” are also strengthening public engagement. This is helping in soil conservation, balanced use of fertilisers and sustainable agricultural practices.

Soil Diversity: Foundation of Agricultural Strength

India’s varied climate, geology, and relief have resulted in a wide range of soils that support region-specific agricultural practices. These soils differ in composition and fertility, enabling diverse cropping patterns and improving resilience across regions.

Alluvial soils are found in the Indo-Gangetic and Brahmaputra plains and the deltas of rivers like Mahanadi, Krishna and Godavari covering states like Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Assam, West Bengal, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh. These soils are fertile and widely distributed across river plains. Black soils in Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, and Gujarat are clayey, retain moisture, and are rich in lime and calcium. Red soils are spread across parts of Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, north-east Andhra Pradesh, eastern Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, West Bengal, and Rajasthan. These soils vary in colour due to iron content and are generally porous and low in key nutrients.

Laterite soils are predominantly found in Eastern Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Kerala, Madhya Pradesh, Odisha, Assam, and parts of Maharashtra. They develop under conditions of high rainfall and intense leaching, which removes soluble nutrients from the soil. Desert soils, in Rajasthan, Gujarat, and Leh-Ladakh, are characterised by their sandy texture, low moisture retention, and the presence of soluble salts. Forest and hill soils are distributed across the Himalayan and North-Eastern regions, varying according to altitude, climate, and vegetation cover.

Saline and alkaline soils are found in the arid and semi-arid regions, coastal and delta regions or areas with prolonged poor drainage and waterlogging. These include parts of Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Haryana, Bihar, Rajasthan, coastal Odisha, the Sundarbans of West Bengal, Kutch region of Gujarat. These soils are characterised by surface salt accumulation, which can affect crop productivity. Marshy soils occur in parts of Tamil Nadu, Bihar, and Uttar Pradesh, where prolonged waterlogging results in high organic matter content. Such wide range of soil types across the country reflects India's diverse agro-climatic landscape and underpins varied cropping systems.

Soil and Climate Action: Building Resilience

Soil plays a critical role in climate change mitigation and adaptation by acting as a significant natural carbon sink. Soils absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and store it as soil organic carbon. This process is facilitated by vegetation growth, roots, and soil microorganisms. The stored carbon can remain in the soil for long periods, helping reduce greenhouse gas concentrations in the atmosphere. In addition, higher soil organic carbon levels improve soil structure, enhance moisture retention capacity, and increase nutrient availability. This further strengthens soil fertility and resilience to climate variability. Moreover, carbon farming is emerging as an important component of climate-smart agriculture. It focuses on removing atmospheric carbon and storing it in soils through sustainable land management practices.

In this context, government interventions support practices that increase soil organic carbon and enhance long-term carbon storage. These include the National Mission for a Green India, National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture, National Mission on Natural Farming, and the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme. Their interventions align closely with the principles of carbon farming.

National Mission for a Green India

The National Mission for a Green India (GIM) is one of the initiatives under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC). It addresses climate change through actions that enhance ecosystem services, particularly carbon storage in forests and trees. Forests play a vital role in climate mitigation by absorbing carbon dioxide and improving natural carbon sinks. The mission focuses on increasing and improving forest and tree cover, including on-forest and degraded lands. This strengthens carbon sinks and supports India’s climate commitments. In pursuance of India’s commitments under its Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC), the mission aims to create an additional carbon sink of 2.5-3.0 billion tonnes of CO₂ equivalent by 2030. As per the India State of Forest Report (ISFR) 2023, an additional carbon sink of 2.29 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent was created between 2005 and 2021, demonstrating significant progress towards this goal. These interventions also enhance soil and biomass carbon while improving ecosystem resilience.

National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC) NAPCC provides a comprehensive framework to address climate change while facilitating sustainable development. It promotes actions that deliver both developmental benefits and climate outcomes, with a focus on adaptation and mitigation. The Plan is implemented through eight national missions. These missions cover areas such as solar energy, water, green India, agriculture, ecosystems and knowledge systems, providing a comprehensive framework for climate action.

National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture

Sustainable agriculture strengthens climate resilience while improving soil health, resource efficiency, and long-term farm productivity. To advance these objectives, in 2014-15, the National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture (NMSA) was launched as a key component of NAPCC. It focuses on promoting sustainable farming through improved soil health, efficient water use and adoption of climate-resilient practices. The mission facilitates farmers through financial assistance and incentives to adopt better soil management and water conservation measures. Since 2014-15, Rs.2,119.84 crore has been released under Rainfed Area Development, covering 8.50 lakh hectares and benefiting 14.35 lakh farmers through Integrated Farming System. These interventions are enhancing soil organic carbon and supporting practices aligned with carbon farming.

National Mission on Natural Farming

The National Mission on Natural Farming, launched in 2024, promotes sustainable agricultural practices through scientifically informed approaches that strengthen soil health and enhance climate resilience. The mission focuses on reducing farmers' input costs while encouraging safe, environmentally responsible food production. It represents a shift towards nature-based farming systems that improve soil organic carbon and support long-term farm viability.

The mission has expanded significantly, with 18,786 clusters covering 8.80 lakh hectares and over 18.19 lakh farmers enrolled as of March 2026. This reflects the growing adoption across States.

Capacity building remains a key component. 33,676 trained Community Resource Persons and a wide network of training institutions support knowledge dissemination on natural farming bio-inputs at the grassroots level.

The mission also contributes to the development of local input systems through Bio-input Resource Centre (BRC) and certification mechanisms. Bio-input Resource Centres are local facilities that supply ready-to-use natural farming inputs such as Beejamrit and Jeevamrit to farmers. They also provide training and demonstrations to farmers to adopt natural alternatives to chemical fertilisers and pesticides. This encourages farmers to adopt sustainable practices while strengthening the overall agricultural ecosystem.

Carbon Credit Trading Scheme

Carbon markets create financial incentives for activities that reduce, avoid, or remove greenhouse gas emissions. The Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, adopted in 2023, enables farmers and organisations to undertake greenhouse gas mitigation activities. It also enables them to earn tradable carbon credit certificates through voluntary carbon markets (VCM). Each carbon credit represents one metric tonne of carbon dioxide equivalent that is reduced, avoided, or removed. Verified standards such as the Verified Carbon Standard (VCS) and Gold Standard provide frameworks for measuring and validating emission reductions in agriculture. By adopting sustainable, eco-friendly practices and obtaining proper verification, farmers can generate carbon credits. They can further sell them to entities seeking emission offsets, thereby enabling climate mitigation while creating an additional income stream.

Integrated Approaches to Soil Health and Fertiliser Management

Soil Health and Fertility Scheme, launched in 2015, promotes scientific soil management by assessing soil nutrient status and guiding farmers on balanced fertiliser use. It provides Soil Health Cards with crop-specific recommendations to improve soil health and productivity. Training and demonstration programmes further support farmers in adopting the judicious use of chemical fertilisers. This includes secondary and micronutrients, along with organic manures and bio-fertilisers. The School Soil Health Programme further extends this effort by engaging students. Students participate in soil sample collection, testing, and preparation of Soil Health Cards. This enables them to support advisory services for farmers.

As of 13 March 2026, a total of 25.89 crore Soil Health Cards have been generated since inception. Around 7.17 lakh demonstrations have been conducted to promote balanced fertiliser use. These demonstrations were conducted through advisories supported by the Agricultural Technology Management Agency (ATMA) and Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs). In addition, 70,002 Krishi Sakhis have been trained to support the dissemination of soil health advisories at the grassroots level. The School Soil Health Programme has registered 4,430 schools and 1,29,167 students in 2025-26.

From Soil Testing to Higher Yields: A Farmer's Experience Shri Nirapado Gorai, a farmer from Dhanbad district, Jharkhand, adopted Soil Health Card recommendations on his 3-acre farm. Prior to this, his nutrient management practices relied largely on nitrogen and phosphorus, with limited use of other essential nutrients. Based on the soil health advisory, he shifted to balanced fertiliser application, including potash and lime. He also applied urea in split doses at different stages of crop growth. This transition reduced fertiliser costs from Rs.1,910 to Rs.1,190 per acre, resulting in savings of Rs.720 per acre. At the same time, paddy yield increased from 1,000 kg to 1,320 kg per acre, along with visible improvement in plant health. Reflecting on the change, he observed less infestation of disease and insect pests. He observed that this was probably due to the judicious use of nitrogenous fertilisers and application of potash.

Balanced Nutrient Management

India has taken significant steps to improve fertiliser management through reforms that promote efficiency and transparency.

The Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) scheme links subsidy support to the nutrient content of fertilisers, encouraging balanced nutrient application. Under this scheme, the government provides subsidies on 28 grades of Phosphatic and Potassic (P&K) fertilisers, including Diammonium Phosphate (DAP), to farmers at subsidised prices through fertiliser manufacturers and importers. The subsidy rates are notified separately for the two major cropping seasons. The Kharif season rates are applicable from 1 April to 30 September, while the Rabi season rates apply from 1 October to 31 March.

Aadhaar-linked Point of Sale (PoS) devices authenticate beneficiaries and enable real-time tracking of fertiliser sales. Under the Direct Benefit Transfer in Fertilisers system, subsidies are released based on actual sales through PoS devices. Fertiliser subsidy disbursements totalled Rs.6.77 lakh crore between 2022-23 and July 2025.

Neem-coated urea slows nitrogen release, improves nutrient-use efficiency, and reduces diversion for non-agricultural purposes. The Government mandated a 100 per cent neem coating on all domestically produced urea.

The Integrated Fertiliser Management System further strengthens monitoring and logistics, ensuring better control over fertiliser availability and use.

Khet Bachao Abhiyan: Promoting Balanced Fertiliser Use

To further strengthen balanced fertiliser use, the Government intensified awareness efforts through the Khet Bachao Abhiyan. The campaign promoted scientific nutrient management practices among farmers.

Led by Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) and the DA&FW, the campaign involved the entire National Agricultural Research, Education and Extension Systems (NAREES) and was held during June 1-30, 2026. It promoted soil test-based nutrient management so as to reduce overdependence on chemical fertilisers through camps, demonstrations, and farmer outreach activities. The campaign also raised awareness of alternative agricultural practices in low-rainfall conditions.

A total of 99,489 interaction meetings and events, 25,178 awareness camps were organised. In addition, 6,721 training programmes and 96,227 field demonstrations on green manuring and bio-fertilisers were conducted to promote balanced nutrient management. The campaign strengthened grassroots participation through 4,916 Janpratinidhi Sammelans, including fertiliser and input dealers. These activities directly engaged around 1.07 crore farmers/other stakeholders, and about 4.5 crore citizens through social media.

These reforms and awareness initiatives are strengthening balanced fertiliser use, improving nutrient efficiency, and supporting long-term soil sustainability.

Building a Digital Soil Ecosystem

India is advancing towards a data-driven approach to soil management by integrating geospatial mapping, digital platforms, and artificial intelligence.

Geospatial Soil Mapping and Resource Assessment

The Nationwide Soil Resource Mapping project, implemented by the Soil and Land Use Survey of India (SLUSI), is generating high-resolution soil profile maps. They combine satellite imagery with field observations to create village-level soil inventories. The inventories support scientific land use planning and informed crop selection.

As of September 2024, around 29 million hectares had been mapped. The target is 142 million hectares of agricultural land. To accelerate this effort, Rs.1,076 crore was provided to six States. These include Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, and Andhra Pradesh. The support is strengthening the creation of reliable soil resource datasets across regions.

Integrating Soil, Land, and Farmer Data

Digital technologies are transforming agricultural decision-making through integrated data systems and targeted advisory services. The Digital Agriculture Mission is building a unified data framework by linking soil information with land and crop datasets. Platforms such as AgriStack are enabling the integration of farmer, land, and soil data to support targeted advisory services. As part of this effort, as of 27 November 2025, over 7.63 crore Farmer IDs have been generated. During Rabi 2024-25, 23.5 crore crop plots were surveyed. This has enabled mapping soil information to individual land parcels, supporting more accurate, location-specific advisories.

AI and Precision Soil Management

Artificial intelligence is transforming soil management by converting complex data into actionable insights for farmers. Under the Digital Agriculture Mission, platforms such as the Krishi Decision Support System (KDSS) integrate soil, weather, and crop data. These platforms generate location-specific advisories for crop planning and nutrient management.

This integration of data and technology is improving soil assessment and supporting informed farm-level decisions.

AI-based soil diagnostics use satellite imagery, remote sensing, and farm-level data to identify nutrient deficiencies. They also detect soil stress and support timely corrective action.

AI-enabled precision farming supports site-specific application of water and fertilisers based on local soil conditions. This improves resource efficiency, reduces input costs, and minimises environmental impacts.

AI-driven advisory systems help farmers make decisions on crop selection, sowing time, and nutrient management.

Advances in agri-robotics are promoting operations such as analysis, planting, and field management. Technologies, including autonomous tractors, use GPS, cameras, and AI for greater precision. Institutions such as ICAR–Indian Agricultural Research Institute are also developing robotic systems for sampling and crop monitoring.

These advancements are enhancing precision, improving productivity, and strengthening the foundation for sustainable and climate-resilient agriculture in India.

Unlocking the Potential Beneath Our Feet

Soil remains one of India’s most valuable natural resources, sustaining agriculture, livelihoods, and food security. Scientific assessment, sustainable farming practices, and improved nutrient management are strengthening its long-term health. Through initiatives such as the Soil Health Card Scheme, NMSA, NMNF, and Green India Mission, the country is promoting scientific and sustainable soil management.

Efforts to improve soil health have encouraged balanced nutrient use, enhanced resource efficiency, carbon storage, and supported climate-resilient farming. At the same time, geospatial mapping, artificial intelligence, and digital agriculture platforms are enabling more precise and data-driven farm decisions. As awareness grows through efforts such as the Khet Bachao Abhiyan, India is steadily unlocking the true potential of soil as a critical national resource.

summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
The press release highlights India's national initiatives promoting scientific soil management, climate-resilient farming, and sustainable agricultural practices to secure soil health and support the farming sector. It details the status of key programs, the country's soil diversity, and the critical role of soil as a carbon sink in climate change mitigation efforts.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
- Agriculture employs over 46 percent of India’s workforce.
- The total reported area of India is 306.65 million hectares, with approximately 59 percent under agriculture.
- The Soil Health Card Scheme has generated over 25.89 crore cards.
- The National Mission on Natural Farming has expanded to 18,786 clusters covering 8.80 lakh hectares.
- India aims to create an additional carbon sink of 2.5-3.0 billion tonnes of CO₂ equivalent by 2030.
- An additional carbon sink of 2.29 billion tonnes of CO₂ equivalent was created between 2005 and 2021.

headline: ENGINEERING SERVICES (MAIN) EXAMINATION, 2026
fullText:
ENGINEERING SERVICES (MAIN) EXAMINATION, 2026

24 JUL 2026 7:01PM by PIB Delhi

On the basis of the result of the written part of the Engineering Services (Main) Examination, 2026 held by U.P.S.C. on 21st June 2026, the candidates with the under mentioned Roll Numbers have qualified for Interview/ Personality Test.

2. The candidature of these candidates is PROVISIONAL subject to their being found eligible in all respects. The candidates would be required to produce the original certificates/documents in support of their claims relating to age, educational qualifications, community, benchmark disability (where applicable) etc. at the time of the Personality Test. They are, therefore, advised to keep their certificates/documents ready before appearance in the Personality Test and check beforehand the requirement of certificates in accordance with the Important Instructions, which are available on the website of the Commission.

3.1 The Commission shall provide a window of 15 (Fifteen) days soon after the declaration of result of Main/Stage-II part of Engineering Services Examination, 2026 to the candidates who qualified for Personality Test/Interview. The candidates, as per Note 2 below Rule 6 of the Rules of Engineering Services Examination, 2026, shall be required to update their details and to upload the proof of passing of the requisite qualifying examination at the specified module/portal, failing which such candidates will not be allowed to appear in the Personality Test/Interview and his/her candidature will be liable to be cancelled. The candidates are advised to check the Commission’s website regularly for this purpose.

3.2 In this period, all the candidates qualified for Personality Test shall also be provided an option to update Correspondence/Postal Address, Higher Qualification, Achievement in different fields, Employment Details/Service Experience, Service Allocation, Service Preferences. The details updated in this window will be treated as final and no request for any change in these fields received through any other mode will be entertained.

3.3 It may be noted that all the candidates qualified for Personality Test/Interview will have to mandatorily login to the portal during this window and update and/or reconfirm their details etc. and submit the Application Form to enable the Commission to generate e-summon letter, failing which such candidates will not be allowed to participate in the further stage of examination. Candidates who have uploaded the required documents/information earlier and have no information to update/fill in are also required to mandatorily login and finally submit after verifying the details so as to generate the e-summon letter for Personality Test of ESE-2026.

4. The instructions for updating the Application Form and the Rules of the Engineering Services Examination, 2026, must be read carefully with regard to the certificates that will be produced at the time of Interview. The candidate will be solely responsible for not producing sufficient proof in support of his/her age, date of birth, educational qualification, community (SC/ST/OBC/EWS), and Certificate of Disability (in the case of PwBD candidate). In case any of the written qualified candidate fails to bring any or all the required original documents in support of his/her candidature for the Engineering Services Examination, 2026, he/she will not be allowed to present himself/herself before the PT Board and no TA will be allowed.

5. The schedule of the Personality Test (Interview) will be informed to the candidates in due course. The exact date of interview will, however, be intimated to the candidates through e-Summon Letter, which may be downloaded from the Commission’s websites https://www.upsc.gov.in and https://www.upsconline.gov.in . A Roll Number-wise Interview Schedule will also be made available on the Commission’s website. The candidates are requested to visit the Commission’s website for updates in this regard.

6. No request for change in the date and time of the Personality Test intimated to the candidates will be entertained under any circumstances.

7. The marks-sheet of all candidates (Qualified & Non-Qualified), will be uploaded on the Commission’s Website after the publication of the final result (i.e. after the conduct of Personality Test) and will remain available on the website for a period of 30 days.

8. The candidates can access the marks-sheets after keying their Roll Numbers and Date of Birth. The authenticated copy of the marks-sheet would, however, be issued by the UPSC to candidates based on specific request accompanied by a self-addressed stamped envelope. The candidates desirous of obtaining printed/hard copies of the marks sheets should make the request within 30 days of the display of the marks on the Commission’s website, beyond which such requests would not be entertained.

9. The candidates may obtain any information/clarification regarding their issue(s) (if any) in respect of the above mentioned Examination on Tel. No. 011- 40303444/24041001 of the Commission’s Call Centre from 09:00 AM to 08:00 PM.

The Union Public Service Commission (U.P.S.C.) announced the results of the written portion of the Engineering Services (Main) Examination, 2026, conducted on 21st June 2026, and declared the candidates eligible for the subsequent Interview/Personality Test.  

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
- **Qualification for Interview**: Candidates with specified Roll Numbers (not listed in the text) have qualified for the Interview/Personality Test, with their candidature marked as provisional pending document verification.

- **Mobile Phones**: Banned in UPSC examination halls.

headline: Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation hosted the 9th edition of Sujal Gram Samvad
fullText:
Ministry of Jal Shakti

Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation hosted the 9th edition of Sujal Gram Samvad

Multilingual dialogue held with 8 Gram Panchayats emphasising GP driven community-led Long-Term water security

24 JUL 2026 6:47PM by PIB Delhi

The Department of Drinking Water & Sanitation (DDWS), Ministry of Jal Shakti, successfully organised the ninth edition of the multilingual ‘Sujal Gram Samvad’ today, reinforcing the government’s commitment to community-led water governance under the Jal Jeevan Mission 2.0.

The virtual Samvad witnessed a diverse range of participants including Gram Panchayat (GP) representatives, members of Village Water and Sanitation Committees (VWSCs), community participants, Jal Sahiyas, Jal Bahinis, Jal Sakhis, Anganwadi workers, students, teachers, and frontline functionaries from the GPs, along with JJM State Mission Directors, District Collectors/District Magistrates/Deputy Commissioners, DWSM officials, and senior officers from the States.

The Samvad was chaired by Shri Ashok K.K. Meena, Secretary, DDWS, in the presence of Shri Kamal Kishore Soan, AS&MD, NJJM, Shri D Senthil Pandiyan, Joint Secretary, NJJM, along with Senior officers from the Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation.

In his opening remarks, Secretary, DDWS, Shri Ashok K.K. Meena, emphasised Sujal Gram Samvad as a platform to learn from Gram Panchayat’s experiences and challenges. He said the best practices shared during the interaction can help other Gram Panchayats strengthen their own systems.

Shri Ashok K. K Meena underlined that drinking water and sanitation are subjects entrusted to Panchayati Raj Institutions under the 73rd Constitutional Amendment and stressed the need for effective devolution of Funds, Functions and Functionaries (3Fs) to Gram Panchayats for sustainable management of rural water supply systems.

He further emphasized that active community ownership through Village Water & Sanitation Committees (VWSCs), particularly with participation of women, youth and ex-servicemen, is essential for ensuring long-term sustainability of rural drinking water infrastructure.

Voices from the Gram Panchayats

GP- Subbegoundenpudur, District- Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu: During the interaction from representatives of GP Subbegoundenpudur in Tamil, they shared that the GP has achieved 100% Har Ghar Jal coverage across villages and habitations, supplying 55 LPCD through surface water drawn from the Bhavani and Aliyar rivers. Community members highlighted the active involvement of Self-Help Groups in water quality testing through Field Test Kits (FTKs) and regular awareness campaigns on safe water use. The GP collects ₹50 per month i.e ₹600 per annum as user charges.

Further, the officials from the District and State informed that the district was declared Har Ghar Jal on 29th June 2024. They are focusing on strengthening Jal Seva Aankalan, source sustainability measures and standard operating procedures for new household tap connections to ensure sustainable service delivery. A dedicated District Source Sustainability Committee has been constituted to protect drinking water sources and ensure long-term sustainability. Around 80,000 PVTG households have been covered under the Mission.

GP- Bariyarpur, District- Lakhisaria, Bihar: The village representatives shared their experiences in Angika language, highlighting water woes prior to JJM and said, many habitations relied on hand pumps and scattered local sources for drinking water. Household tap connections have significantly reduced the burden on women and improved access to safe

drinking water. The interaction also highlighted the district's focus on extending piped water supply to remote tribal habitations and strengthening community participation in O&M.

District officials informed that surveys have been completed in uncovered tribal hamlets and piped water supply schemes are being implemented in a phased manner. They also highlighted regular DWSM meetings and the gradual transfer of completed schemes to Gram Panchayats for sustainable management.

GP- Sonamarg B, District- Ganderbal, Jammu & Kashmir: During the interaction with the village representatives of GP Sonamarg in Hindi, it was informed that the Gram Panchayat receives drinking water through gravity-based spring water supply schemes, which have substantially improved drinking water availability in the mountainous region. They informed that the community actively participates in protection and rejuvenation of natural springs, while youth groups support environmental conservation and cleanliness around tourist areas. The villagers also highlighted that community ownership has strengthened operation and maintenance of the schemes. Being Himalayan region earlier they used to rely on stream water but under JJM even in harsh winter they are getting uninterrupted water because of the insulated pipe water supply technology.

District officials informed that the district has undertaken rejuvenation of nearly 30 springs, prepared District Improvement Plans (DIP) for sustainable water security and continues to prioritise source sustainability, gravity-fed systems and convergence with other programmes and local institutions.

GP- Lallen, District-Mamit, Mizoram: Interaction with the village members of Mamit was carried out in Mizo language. The beneficiaries reported that the VWSC has established an effective community-led operation and maintenance system. Leakages are reported immediately through WhatsApp groups, while locally engaged plumbers and pump operators attend complaints promptly. Community members highlighted regular awareness campaigns on water conservation and responsible water use.

The District Collector informed that DWSM meetings are being held regularly and proceedings are uploaded. Convergence with the Forest Department, Rural Development and Watershed programmes is being strengthened to improve source sustainability, particularly in hilly areas where natural water sources are gradually drying up.

GP- Etori, District- Shahjahanpur, Uttar Pradesh: The village representatives shared their experiences in Hindi. Household tap water connections under JJM have significantly improved access to safe drinking water, reducing the drudgery of fetching water and improving the health and well-being of the community. They highlighted that the GP regularly discusses O&M of the water supply system in Gram Sabha meetings, promotes responsible water use and water conservation, and encourages community participation in ensuring the long-term sustainability of the scheme. Village representatives also shared that awareness campaigns are being organised, and the GP is promoting timely reporting and resolution of water supply-related issues.

Further District Administration informed that special efforts are being undertaken to provide safe drinking water to Koda and Kora tribal habitations, ensuring that even the most remote and vulnerable communities receive safe drinking water through household tap water connections. They also highlighted regular DWSM meetings, Jan Chaupal programmes for community awareness, and focused orientation of Gram Panchayats on the Jal Sarthi App.

GP- Faridpur, District- Panipat, Haryana: The village representatives shared their experiences in Haryanvi language and said that the GP has developed a strong community-led O&M model. A monthly user charge is collected from households and utilised for routine repairs, while locally appointed pump operators and plumbers ensure immediate rectification of leakages and other faults. Monthly ₹13,000 total revenue is collected towards user charge. Self-Help Group women regularly undertake water quality testing through FTKs, while school children, ASHA workers and Anganwadi workers conduct awareness campaigns on water conservation and safe water practices. The GP also uses WhatsApp groups and monthly community meetings for grievance redressal and timely maintenance.

District and State officials highlighted Haryana's efforts to implement the State Water Policy by onboarding SHG women for user charge collection, water quality monitoring and grievance redressal, thereby strengthening community ownership of rural water supply schemes. Faridpur GP is the pioneer in implementing the O&M policy.

GP- Husir, District- Khunti, Jharkhand: The village representatives shared their experiences in Sadri language and informed that JJM has brought a significant transformation in the predominantly tribal Gram Panchayat by providing household tap water connections, eliminating the need to fetch water from distant streams and improving health. Gram Sabha plays an active role in planning and overseeing the O&M of scheme. A local pump operator has been engaged to ensure regular water supply, while the GP has adopted a community-led user charge mechanism, collecting ₹30 per month per household, with economically weaker families being exempted. The Panchayat collects around ₹9,400 every month, which is utilised for payment of the pump operator, plumber, carrying out routine repairs and addressing pipeline leakages, thereby ensuring uninterrupted water supply and strengthening community ownership of the scheme.

District officials informed that a structured monitoring mechanism has been instituted under which district and block-level officers have been assigned villages for regular field visits, with every 4th Saturday marked for reviewing implementation of JJM and resolving field-level issues. They further shared that the district is working to expand community ownership beyond the VWSC by partnering with Large Area Multi-Purpose Cooperative Societies (LAMPS), Primary Agricultural Credit Societies (PACS) and Self-Help Groups (SHGs) to strengthen operation and maintenance, user charge collection and long-term sustainability of rural drinking water systems.

GP- Kotdhi, District- Balrampur, Chhattisgarh: The village representatives shared their experiences in Hindi and highlighted that JJM has significantly improved access to safe drinking water while reducing the burden of fetching water from distant sources. They informed that drinking water quality is monitored every fortnight using FTKs by trained community members. The test results are promptly shared with villagers through a dedicated WhatsApp group, ensuring transparency and enhancing community awareness regarding water quality. They further shared that soak pits have been constructed for the safe disposal of greywater, contributing to improved sanitation and environmental sustainability. Regular awareness campaigns are also conducted to promote responsible water use and encourage community participation in the maintenance of village water supply assets. The GPs are leveraging 15th FC grants to support the O&M of rural water supply schemes.

District and State officials highlighted the initiatives being undertaken for water conservation through convergence with VB-GRAM G and other programmes. They also shared various local development interventions aimed at strengthening source sustainability and ensuring the long-term sustainability of rural drinking water infrastructure.

AS & MD, NJJM, Shri Kamal Kishore Soan, expressed appreciation to the active support of District Collectors and Gram Panchayats for their commitment towards JJM.

He urged for effective utilisation the 16th FC grants for which guidelines will be share soon. He emphasised the need for regular DWSM meetings to review the functionality of schemes, address implementation challenges and monitor service delivery. Stressing that source sustainability, he called upon all stakeholders to undertake sustained efforts for protecting and rejuvenating drinking water sources so that future generations continue to receive uninterrupted piped water supply.

He further urged District Collectors and District Magistrates to actively participate in Jal Arpan programmes and ensure that all prescribed protocols are followed, thereby promoting community ownership and accountability.

Addressing the Gram Pradhans, Shri Soan encouraged them to regularly review the status of their village water supply schemes on the e-Gram Swaraj Portal, use the information for informed decision-making, and proactively share the status with the community through Gram Sabha meetings to strengthen transparency, community participation and collective responsibility for sustainable rural drinking water services.

The Samvad started by setting the context and sharing objective by Shri Y K Singh, Director, NJJM. He said that the Samvad aims to listen village communities, understand their experiences and learn local practices on O&M, and source sustenance.

headline: Mine closure is a landmark initiative in India's transition towards circular economy: Dr Jitendra Singh
fullText:
Ministry of Science & Technology

Mine closure is a landmark initiative in India's transition towards circular economy: Dr Jitendra Singh

India's Mine Closure Journey Demonstrates Science Can Transform Environmental Challenges into Development Opportunities: Dr Jitendra Singh

India's Scientific Mine Closure Model Can Inspire Sustainable Development Across Sectors: Dr Jitendra Singh

Scientific Mine Closure Strengthens India's Journey Towards Net Zero and Sustainable Development: Dr Jitendra Singh

24 JUL 2026 6:34PM by PIB Delhi

Describing the Mine closure as is a landmark initiative in India's transition towards circular economy, Union Minister of State (Independent Charge) for Science & Technology, Earth Sciences and Minister of State for the Prime Minister's Office, Personnel, Public Grievances, Pensions, Atomic Energy and Space, Dr. Jitendra Singh, today said that India is redefining the very idea of mine closure by transforming exhausted mines into new centres of wealth creation, environmental restoration and sustainable livelihoods.

Referring to scientific mine closure as an embodiment of Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi's vision of innovation-led governance, Dr. Jitendra Singh said a closed mine should no longer be viewed as the end of economic activity but as the beginning of a new development journey that serves both people and the planet.

Dr. Jitendra Singh was addressing the release of "AAROH- Annual Report on Mine Closure", organised by the Ministry of Coal in New Delhi. The event was graced by Union Minister for Coal and Mines Shri G. Kishan Reddy, Minister of State for Coal and Mines Shri Satish Chandra Dubey, Secretary, Ministry of Coal Vikram Dev Dutt, senior officials of the Ministry, representatives of coal companies, industry leaders and international partners. The programme also witnessed the virtual inauguration of Coal NEER Plants, signing of the Implementation Agreement between the Ministry of Coal and GIZ for technical cooperation on closed coal mines, and Tripartite MoUs for establishing vocational training centres to create sustainable livelihood opportunities in coal-bearing regions.

Calling the initiative a landmark in India's transition towards a circular economy, Dr. Jitendra Singh said scientific mine closure simultaneously advances several national priorities, including waste-to-wealth, resource efficiency, environmental conservation, sustainable land use and community development. "You have closed a coal mine and opened another mine of wealth," he remarked, describing the initiative as a powerful example of how science and technology can unlock fresh economic opportunities from assets that had long been considered exhausted.

The Minister said such transformative thinking has become possible because the present Government encourages innovation instead of remaining confined to conventional approaches. He said Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi has consistently inspired institutions to challenge established practices and adopt bold, future-oriented solutions capable of delivering long-term national gains. Scientific mine closure, he said, represents one such policy innovation where environmental responsibility has been converted into an instrument of economic growth and social empowerment.

Dr. Jitendra Singh said the initiative complements India's commitment to achieve Net Zero by 2070 by ensuring that environmental restoration progresses alongside economic development. He noted that as India expands renewable energy, nuclear energy and other emerging technologies, responsible utilisation and regeneration of natural resources will become equally important pillars of sustainable development. Progress, he said, must be measured not only by the efficiency with which resources are utilised but also by the responsibility with which they are restored for future generations.

Appreciating the transformation documented through the 42 scientifically closed mines featured in AAROH, Dr. Jitendra Singh said these examples establish a practical roadmap for the future of post-mining land use in India. He said reclaimed mining areas can evolve into centres of economic activity by supporting agriculture, renewable energy, tourism, skill development and other community-centric initiatives. He also suggested expanding participation of self-help groups, particularly women's groups, so that scientific mine closure generates sustainable livelihood opportunities while strengthening local economies.

Welcoming the Implementation Agreement between the Ministry of Coal and Germany's GIZ, Dr. Jitendra Singh said sustainable development increasingly depends upon collaborative partnerships that combine global expertise with local innovation. He described the agreement as a model capable of bringing together governments, scientific institutions and industry to accelerate scientific mine closure and land repurposing. Such collaborative frameworks, he said, have the potential to become replicable models across multiple sectors of the economy.

The Minister stressed that successful public initiatives should be communicated in ways that inspire wider public participation. Referring to the visual documentation presented during the programme, he suggested preparing short digital films on each scientifically reclaimed mine so that citizens, administrators and stakeholders across the country can witness these transformations firsthand. Such storytelling, he said, would enable India's mine closure experience to become a national learning resource while encouraging similar innovations in other sectors.

Drawing upon successful initiatives from other sectors, Dr. Jitendra Singh referred to the productive use of steel slag in road construction and the conversion of used cooking oil into biofuel, both of which initially appeared unconventional but eventually evolved into valuable economic resources. These experiences, he said, demonstrate that science-driven innovation has the power to convert waste into wealth while simultaneously generating public awareness, environmental benefits and new economic opportunities. He said the same philosophy lies at the heart of scientific mine closure, where yesterday's exhausted assets become tomorrow's engines of sustainable growth.

The Ministry of Coal has been implementing a comprehensive framework for scientific mine closure that integrates ecological restoration, community development and sustainable post-mining land use. The AAROH- Annual Report on Mine Closure captures India's journey in scientifically closing 42 mines through detailed case studies, before-and-after documentation and digital resources, while showcasing best practices for future mine closure initiatives. Complementing these efforts are the revised Mine Closure Guidelines, the RECLAIM, LIVES and SUVIKALP frameworks, Coal NEER plants for productive utilisation of mine water, and partnerships aimed at strengthening international cooperation and community-centric development. Together, these initiatives reflect the Government's commitment to ensuring that every phase of mining contributes to sustainable and inclusive national development.

headline: Over 13,550 Scheduled Caste Students in Karnataka's Chikkaballapura District Benefit Under Central Scholarship Schemes: MoS for Social Justice and Empowerment Shri Ramdas Athawale in Written Reply in Lok Sabha
fullText:
Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment

Over 13,550 Scheduled Caste Students in Karnataka's Chikkaballapura District Benefit Under Central Scholarship Schemes: MoS for Social Justice and Empowerment Shri Ramdas Athawale in Written Reply in Lok Sabha

8,460 Students Receive Post-Matric Scholarship While 5,090 Students Benefit Under Pre-Matric Scholarship During Academic Year 2025–26

24 JUL 2026 6:29PM by PIB Delhi

The Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment is continuously strengthening the implementation of Centrally Sponsored Scholarship Schemes to ensure equitable access to quality education for Scheduled Caste (SC) students across the country. Through technology-enabled service delivery, Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) and sustained awareness initiatives, the Ministry is ensuring timely financial assistance, enhanced transparency and efficient delivery of scholarship benefits to eligible students.

This information was given by Minister of State for Social Justice and Empowerment Shri Ramdas Athawale in a written reply to Dr. K Sudhakar in the Lok Sabha.

The Minister informed the House that the Department is implementing three major scholarship schemes for Scheduled Caste students, namely the Post-Matric Scholarship for Scheduled Castes (PMS-SC), the Pre-Matric Scholarship for Scheduled Castes and Others, and the SHREYAS – Scholarships for Higher Education for Young Achievers Scheme for Scheduled Castes. These schemes aim to reduce financial barriers and promote access to quality education among Scheduled Caste students.

During the Academic Year 2025–26, 8,460 students from Chikkaballapura district of Karnataka benefited under the Post-Matric Scholarship Scheme, while 5,090 students received assistance under the Pre-Matric Scholarship Scheme, taking the total number of beneficiaries to 13,550. No beneficiary from the district availed assistance under the SHREYAS-SC Scheme during the same period.

The Minister further informed that the Ministry introduced the Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) mechanism under the Post-Matric Scholarship Scheme in 2021–22 and under the Pre-Matric Scholarship Scheme in 2022–23. The DBT system has significantly improved scholarship delivery by ensuring direct transfer of funds into Aadhaar-seeded bank accounts of beneficiaries, eliminating duplication, reducing delays and enhancing transparency in fund disbursement. The technology-driven system has particularly benefited students residing in rural and semi-urban areas by ensuring timely receipt of financial assistance.

To ensure that eligible students are fully aware of the available scholarship schemes, the Department has undertaken extensive awareness and outreach activities across the country. These include advertisements in national and regional newspapers, social media campaigns, continuous engagement with States and Union Territories, regular monitoring through National Chintan Shivirs, regional review meetings and national conferences with State Governments and other stakeholders to improve coverage and implementation of the schemes.

The written reply further stated that while the Department has not independently assessed changes in dropout rates among Scheduled Caste students at the higher secondary and graduation levels, an evaluation conducted by NITI Aayog has observed that the scholarship schemes have played a significant role in promoting educational equity by reducing financial constraints and encouraging continuation of higher education among students belonging to disadvantaged communities. The evaluation also noted an improvement in scholarship disbursement following the introduction of the DBT system.

Regarding revision of maintenance allowance under the scholarship schemes, the Minister informed the House that any revision is undertaken in consultation with all concerned stakeholders in accordance with the prescribed Government procedures.

The Government remains committed to empowering Scheduled Caste students through inclusive and transparent scholarship programmes that promote educational opportunities, reduce financial barriers and strengthen social justice. Through sustained reforms, digital governance and continuous engagement with States and stakeholders, the Ministry continues to ensure that no eligible student is deprived of educational assistance due to financial constraints.

PR – 030– TAR/KV/GA

(Release ID: 2289063)

headline: Union MoS Dr. Chandra Sekhar Pemmasani Inaugurates Modernised Jan Seva Connect Post Offices in Guntur
fullText:
Ministry of Communications

Union MoS Dr. Chandra Sekhar Pemmasani Inaugurates Modernised Jan Seva Connect Post Offices in Guntur

₹5,785-Crore Digital Transformation, AI-Powered Operations and Corporate Reforms Propel India Post into a New Era

Andhra Pradesh Postal Circle Emerges as the Nation's Best Performer in Q1 FY 2026–27

24 JUL 2026 6:24PM by PIB Delhi

Union Minister of State for Communications and Rural Development, Dr. Chandra Sekhar Pemmasani, today inaugurated the modernised Kothapeta and Chandramouli Nagar Sub-Post Offices in Guntur under the 'Jan Seva Connect' initiative. Designed as next-generation citizen service centres, the renovated post offices combine modern infrastructure with the rich cultural heritage of Andhra Pradesh, offering integrated postal, banking, digital and logistics services under one roof.

Addressing the gathering, Dr. Chandrasekhar said that India Post is no longer merely a department that delivers letters but a national institution connecting lives, empowering communities and driving India's digital and economic transformation. Under the leadership of Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi and the guidance of Union Minister for Communications Shri Jyotiraditya M. Scindia, the Department of Posts is undergoing the most comprehensive transformation in its 170-year history, positioning itself as a world-class logistics, e-commerce and financial services network.

The Minister of State said the Government has launched one of the country's largest technology modernisation programmes in the postal sector with an investment of ₹5,785 crore under IT 2.0 and Advanced Postal Technology (APT) 2.0. The initiative is integrating Artificial Intelligence (AI), cloud-based platforms and real-time data across the country's network of more than 1.65 lakh post offices, nearly 90 per cent of which are located in rural India. The transformation is enabling faster service delivery, real-time tracking, improved operational efficiency and a seamless customer experience.

Dr. Chandrasekhar said India Post has also embraced a corporate-style governance model by introducing strategic leadership positions such as Chief Technology Officer (CTO), Chief Marketing Officer (CMO) and Vice CTO. With clearly defined business targets, monthly performance reviews, accountability frameworks and a customer-first approach, the Department is becoming more agile, competitive and responsive to the evolving needs of citizens and businesses.

Highlighting the transformation in logistics, the Minister of State said India Post has introduced revised pricing, bulk discounts, Buy Now Pay Later (BNPL) facilities, special packaging solutions, geotagged deliveries, SMS tracking, OTP-based delivery confirmation and 24-to-48-hour delivery standards. Strategic partnerships with ONDC, GeM, Amazon, Flipkart, Patanjali and international logistics providers such as DHL are significantly expanding India's e-commerce ecosystem and strengthening India Post's position in the logistics market.

He said post offices are rapidly evolving into integrated citizen service centres by offering UPI payments, Aadhaar enrolment and updation services, Passport Seva facilities, India Post Payments Bank doorstep banking, Postal Life Insurance, Rural Postal Life Insurance and government-backed savings schemes. India Post currently manages over 3.8 crore Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana accounts, while more than 60 per cent of postal transactions are now conducted digitally, reflecting the Department's successful transition towards a cash-lite, technology-enabled ecosystem.

The Minister of State described Gramin Dak Sevaks (GDS) and postmen as the backbone of this transformation. Through GDS Sammelans, structured training programmes, sales orientation, digital capacity building, grievance monitoring, recognition mechanisms and performance-linked accountability, India Post is empowering its frontline workforce to become trusted ambassadors of financial inclusion and digital governance in rural India. He said GDS employees are playing a crucial role in helping families understand savings, insurance and financial planning while expanding access to government services.

Dr. Chandrasekhar noted that these reforms are already producing remarkable results. During the first quarter of FY 2026–27, India Post recorded its highest-ever first-quarter revenue of over ₹4,000 crore, reaching ₹4,009 crore and registering a 22 per cent year-on-year growth. Zero-transaction accounts have declined by more than 90 per cent, while the Effective Capability Ratio (ECR) has improved from 41 per cent to 47 per cent, reflecting significant gains in operational efficiency.

Congratulating the Andhra Pradesh Postal Circle, the MoS said the Circle has emerged as the best-performing Postal Circle in the country during the first quarter of FY 2026–27. Andhra Pradesh achieved 102 per cent of its quarterly business target, generating ₹233.7 crore in revenue, secured the No. 1 national ranking in Mail Operations, recorded an impressive 140 per cent growth in parcel business, and generated over ₹50 crore under the 'Jayaho Bheema Sankalp' insurance campaign. He also noted that nearly 33 per cent of the beneficiary payments under major Andhra Pradesh Government welfare schemes, including Talliki Vandanam, Deepam, NTR Rythu Bharosa and NTR Nagar, are being delivered through the postal network, highlighting its unmatched last-mile reach.

Speaking about the Guntur projects, Dr. Chandrasekhar said the two post offices have been redesigned under the guidance of the School of Planning and Architecture to celebrate Andhra Pradesh's cultural identity. The Kothapeta Sub-Post Office, modernised at a cost of ₹51.81 lakh, features a distinctive red feature wall inspired by the 1975 Kuchipudi commemorative postage stamp, while the Chandramouli Nagar Sub-Post Office, renovated at a cost of ₹64.13 lakh, draws architectural inspiration from the iconic Ananda Nilayam tower of Tirumala.

The Minister of State congratulated the officers and staff of the Andhra Pradesh Postal Circle and Guntur Division for successfully implementing the Jan Seva Connect initiative. He conveyed special appreciation to the postmen and Gramin Dak Sevaks, describing them as the real heroes behind India Post's success for delivering essential services to every household, irrespective of weather or geography.

Concluding his address, Dr. Chandrasekhar said that Viksit Bharat is not merely a Government mission but a shared national responsibility. He reaffirmed that the Centre and the Andhra Pradesh Government are working in close partnership to ensure faster, technology-driven and citizen-centric governance. He expressed confidence that every post office under the Jan Seva Connect initiative would emerge as a trusted public institution supporting students, empowering entrepreneurs, serving women, assisting senior citizens and strengthening communities while taking India another step closer to the vision of a Developed India.

"The Postal Department is not just about carrying letters—it is a bridge that connects millions of lives and reaches every corner of our state."

Inaugurated the newly renovated Kothapeta Sub-Post Office today alongside Guntur East MLA Shri Mohammed Naseer Ahmed .

Following the… pic.twitter.com/MHD8YJ1Xqr

— Dr. Chandra Sekhar Pemmasani (@PemmasaniOnX) July 24, 2026

Delighted to inaugurate the updated Chandramouli Nagar Sub-Post Office today with MLC Alapati Rajendra Prasad along with Guntur West MLA Smt @galla_madhavi Garu under the #JanSevaConnect initiative.

Guided by the visionary leadership of Hon’ble PM Shri @narendramodi Ji and… pic.twitter.com/pnpTYaLOCC

headline: Ayushman Arogya Shivir Concludes Successfully Across Three Kartavya Bhawans in New Delhi
fullText:
Ministry of Health and Family Welfare

Ayushman Arogya Shivir Concludes Successfully Across Three Kartavya Bhawans in New Delhi

Over 1700 Government Officials and Staff from Over 16 Ministries Avail Preventive Health Screening and Medical Consultation

Ni-kshay Vahans Equipped with Advanced Diagnostic Facilities Enable On-site TB Screening and Early Detection

24 JUL 2026 6:06PM by PIB Delhi

The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare successfully organised a three-day Ayushman Arogya Shivir from 22 to 24 July 2026 across Kartavya Bhawan-I, Kartavya Bhawan-II and Kartavya Bhawan-III in New Delhi. Organised by the Central TB Division (CTD) in collaboration with the Delhi State TB Cell, National TB Elimination Programme (NTEP) and Swasth Nagrik Abhiyaan (SNA) Division of the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, the initiative aimed to promote preventive healthcare, facilitate early diagnosis and strengthen awareness on healthy living among Government officials and Central Secretariat staff working in the Kartavya Bhawans.

The Ayushman Arogya Shivir commenced at Kartavya Bhawan-I on 22nd July 2026, where it was inaugurated by Smt. Aradhana Patnaik, Additional Secretary & Mission Director, Ministry of Health & Family Welfare. During the inauguration, she emphasised the importance of prioritising preventive healthcare and encouraging individuals to take proactive steps towards their health and well-being. The health camp was subsequently organised at Kartavya Bhawan-II on 23rd July, where Dr. Shobini Rajan, Deputy Director General, Central TB Division, interacted with participants and highlighted the importance of early detection, timely diagnosis and preventive healthcare in achieving the vision of a TB Mukt Bharat. The Shivir culminated at Kartavya Bhawan-III on 24th July 2026, ensuring wider outreach among Government officials and employees across the three office complexes.

The three-day initiative witnessed an encouraging response, with over 1700 Government officials and other staff from over 16 Ministries availing free health screening and medical consultation services. Comprehensive health assessments included Blood Pressure (BP) measurement, blood sugar testing, Body Mass Index (BMI) assessment, Haemoglobin (Hb) estimation, Chest X-ray screening, medical consultation and referral services, wherever required. Officers, security personnel, housekeeping staff, sanitation workers and other employees actively participated in the health camp, reaffirming the growing emphasis on preventive healthcare in the workplace.

A major highlight of the Shivir was the deployment of two Ni-kshay Vahans equipped with Digital Chest X-ray, CBNAAT and TrueNat diagnostic facilities. These mobile diagnostic units enabled on-site TB screening, molecular testing and prompt referral of suspected cases, thereby strengthening efforts towards early detection and timely treatment under the National TB Elimination Programme (NTEP).

The successful organisation of the health camp was supported by a dedicated team of around 60 healthcare providers, including doctors, specialists, laboratory personnel, radiographers, nursing staff, programme officials, TB Vijetas and My Bharat Volunteers, whose coordinated efforts ensured seamless delivery of healthcare services and effective awareness generation throughout the three-day initiative.

The Ayushman Arogya Shivir reflects the Government of India's continued commitment to strengthening preventive healthcare, promoting early diagnosis and expanding access to quality health services. Such workplace-based initiatives contribute to the vision of Viksit Bharat, Healthy India and TB Mukt Bharat by encouraging regular health check-ups, fostering health-seeking behaviour and facilitating early identification and treatment of diseases among Government employees.

headline: PROMOTION OF AI USE IN AGRICULTURE AND IMPACT ASSESSMENT
fullText:
Ministry of Agriculture &

PROMOTION OF AI USE IN AGRICULTURE AND IMPACT ASSESSMENT

24 JUL 2026 6:01PM by PIB Delhi

The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare has initiated several AI-related projects. These are being used for farmer advisories, pest control, crop identification, insurance, delivery of scheme benefits, and governance analytics. Under the Digital Agriculture Mission, the Government also supports the States for projects involving modern digital technology, such as Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning, Block chain Technology, the Internet of Things, Robotics, etc. Some key AI initiatives of the government are as follows:

National Pest Surveillance System (NPSS) – This system tackles the loss of produce due to climate change, utilizes AI and Machine Learning to detect pest infestation in crop issues, enabling timely intervention for healthier crops. This tool, currently used by over 10,000 extension workers, allows farmers to capture images of pests to help them mitigate pest attacks and reduce crop losses. At present, it supports 73 crops over 436 pests.

AI-based Monsoon and Weather Advisory: The India Meteorological Department (IMD) provides AI-based monsoon and weather advisories to support informed agricultural decision-making. The advisories enable farmers to plan agricultural operations based on timely and location-specific weather forecasts. As on date, the initiative has reached approximately 5.28 crore farmers.

BharatVistaar (Virtually Integrated System to Access Agricultural Resources) is a multilingual Artificial Intelligence (AI)-powered Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) for Agriculture. The platform provides farmers with AI-enabled, real-time and location-specific agricultural advisories and access to Government schemes and agricultural support services.

As on date, the platform has served over 3 lakh farmers and addressed more than 72 lakh farmer queries. It provides information on 18 Central Government schemes, crop and livestock advisories, weather forecasts, mandi prices, pest and disease identification, soil health-based recommendations and grievance redressal.

The platform is available through a web portal, mobile application, AI chatbot and dedicated telephone line. At present, BharatVistaar Telephone service is available in 2 languages, i.e. Hindi and English; however, the chatbot, web portal, and mobile application are available in 10 languages.

No specific assessment has been undertaken by the Government to comprehensively evaluate the impact of AI-based technologies. The funds allocated and utilised for digital agriculture initiatives, including AI-based agricultural initiatives, during the last five years are as under:

Year | 2022-23 | 2023-24 | 2024-25 | 2025-26 | 2026-27

Funds Allocated | 60 | 450 | 763.88 | 758.56 | 502.40

Funds Utilized | 20.84 | 119.44 | 356 | 596.10 | 364.62

Further, the Government also provides technical assistance and financial support for human resources to the States/UTs for implementing the projects and capacity-building to enhance farmers' access to AI-enabled technologies and digital services, including small and marginal farmers.

This information was given by the Union Minister of state for Agriculture and Farmers welfare, SHRI RAMNATH THAKUR in a written reply in the Rajya Sabha.

headline: ROLLOUT OF WEATHER INFORMATION NETWORK DATA SYSTEM
fullText:
Ministry of Agriculture &

ROLLOUT OF WEATHER INFORMATION NETWORK DATA SYSTEM

24 JUL 2026 6:01PM by PIB Delhi

Weather Information Network and Data System (WINDS) is an initiative of Government aimed at developing a national level network of Automatic Weather Stations (AWS) and Automatic Rain Gauges (ARGs) for generation of long term hyperlocal weather data for crop insurance, agriculture advisories and disaster risk resilience needs of different sectors. WINDS has been adopted by 7 States/UTs (Assam, Himachal Pradesh, Kerala, Odisha, Puducherry, Uttar Pradesh and Uttarakhand) wherein as on date, 16,843 sites have been approved for installation, civil work has been completed in 1,188 site and 892 AWS/ ARGs have been installed.

The PMFBY envisages adoption of improved technology in implementation of the scheme. Accordingly, Yield Estimation System based on Technology (YES-TECH) is being implemented with effect from Kharif 2023 for objective crop damage, loss assessment. YES-TECH envisages gradual migration to remote sensing based yield estimation to help in fair and accurate crop yield estimation. The initiative has been launched for paddy and wheat crops wef Kharif 2023 and for Soybean crop wef Kharif 2024 season wherein mandatory 30% weightage to yield estimation has been assigned to YES-TECH derived yield.

YES-TECH was implemented in 12 States across 344 Districts in 2025-26. Further, State Government of Madhya Pradesh has given 100% weightage to technology based yield, whereas State Governments of Maharashtra and Uttar Pradesh has given 50% weightage while Odisha and Karnataka State Governments have given 40% weightage to YES-TECH based yield in 2025-26.

Government has a mechanism of rigorous review with stakeholders on effective YES-TECH implementation.

Following steps have been taken by Govt. of India to for timely settlement of claims under the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana:

Government has undertaken development of National Crop Insurance Portal (NCIP) as a single source of data for subsidy payment, dissemination of information, delivery of services including online enrollment of farmers, uploading/obtaining individual insured famer’s details and transfer of claim amount electronically to the individual farmer’s Bank Account.

In order to rigorously monitor claim disbursal process, a dedicated module namely ‘Digiclaim Module’ has been operationalized for payment of claims from Kharif 2022 onwards. It involves integration of National Crop Insurance Portal (NCIP) with Public Finance Management System (PFMS) and accounting system of Insurance Companies to provide timely & transparent processing of all claims.

W.e.f. Kharif 2024, in case payment is not made timely by Insurance Company, penalty.of 12% is auto-calculated and levied through NCIP.

Delinking of Central Government share of premium subsidy from that of State Governments has been implemented so that farmers can get proportionate claims relating to the Central Government share.

12% penalty is also levied from Kharif 2025 on State Government for delay in release of State share of subsidy.

Opening of ESCROW Account by the State Government concerned for deposit of their premium share in advance as per provisions of the scheme has been made mandatory w.e.f. Kharif 2025 season for bringing financial discipline.

Also, towards leveraging technology in implementation of the scheme, various steps like capturing of yield data/Crop Cutting Experiments (CCEs) data through CCE-Agri App & uploading it on the NCIP, allowing insurance companies to witness the conduct of CCEs, integration of State land records with NCIP and usage of remote sensing based yield estimation through YES-TECH have already been taken to improve timely settlement of the claims to farmers.

Crop Loss Assessment App (CLAP), developed as a digital mobile application to streamline and improve the efficiency of crop loss assessments for loacalised calamities/post harvest losses for individual farms and land parcels under PMFBY. The use of CLAP has been made mandatory for all the States/UTs under the scheme.

This information was given by the Union Minister of state for Agriculture and Farmers welfare, SHRI RAMNATH THAKUR in a written reply in the Rajya Sabha.

headline: NHRC, India takes suo motu cognizance of the reported sexual harassment of girl students by the Principal of a government school in Moga district, Punjab
fullText:
National Human Rights Commission

NHRC, India takes suo motu cognizance of the reported sexual harassment of girl students by the Principal of a government school in Moga district, Punjab

Issues notices to the state Chief Secretary and Moga SSP, calling for a detailed report on the matter within two weeks

24 JUL 2026 5:59PM by PIB Delhi

The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC), India has taken suo motu cognizance of a media report that the Principal of a government school in Moga district, Punjab sexually harassed several girl students by inappropriately touching them. Reportedly, he used to threaten the students that he would fail them in the examination, if they complained against him in this regard.

The Commission has observed that the contents of the news report, if true, raise serious issues of human rights violation. Therefore, it has issued notices to the state Chief Secretary and Moga Senior Superintendent of Police, calling for a detailed report on the matter within two weeks.

According to the media report, carried on 23rd July 2026, the incident came to light when one of the victims informed her parents. The parents of the aggrieved girl called the Village Panchayat and reached the school to confront the Principal, who denied the allegation. However, several other girl students came forward making similar charges against him. The Police was also called.

(Release ID: 2289029)

headline: PROGRESS OF NATURAL FARMING UNDER THE NATIONAL MISSION FOR NATURAL FARMING
fullText:

PROGRESS OF NATURAL FARMING UNDER THE NATIONAL MISSION FOR NATURAL FARMING

24 JUL 2026 5:59PM by PIB Delhi

The Union Cabinet approved the National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF) on 25th November 2024 as a centrally sponsored scheme which is being implemented across the country to promote natural farming. Natural Farming is a chemical free farming, involving livestock integrated natural farming methods and diversified crop systems rooted in the Indian traditional knowledge. Natural farming improves soil health, restores ecosystems and reduces input cost to the farmer to achieve greater climate resilience. Natural farming recognises the interdependence of the natural ecosystem amongst soil, water, microbiome, plants, animals, climate and human requirements. headline: CLIMATE RISK AND CROP INSURANCE
fullText:
Ministry of Agriculture &

CLIMATE RISK AND CROP INSURANCE

24 JUL 2026 5:58PM by PIB Delhi

The Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) and Restructured Weather Based Crop Insurance Scheme (RWBCIS) is mainly implemented on ‘Area Approach’ basis wherein claims are worked out on the area of the land parcel based on the shortfall in actual yield of the crop insured, submitted by the State Government concerned, on the basis of Crop Cutting Experiment (CCE) as well as technology based yield estimated under YES-TECH (wherever applicable), vis-à-vis threshold yield, as per the formula in the Operational Guidelines of the scheme.

However, losses due to localized risks of hailstorm, landslide, inundation, cloud burst & natural fire and post-harvest losses due to cyclone, cyclonic/unseasonal rains & hailstorms are calculated on individual insured farm basis under PMFBY and RWBCIS. The extent of loss and claims in such case are assessed by a joint committee comprising representatives of State Government and concerned insurance company within stipulated timeframe under the Operational Guidelines of the scheme.

Further, as per the National Policy on Disaster Management (NPDM), the State Governments undertake relief measures in the wake of natural calamities from funds available in the form of the State Disaster Response Fund (SDRF) in accordance with the Government of India approved items and norms. Additional financial assistance, over and above SDRF, is considered from the National Disaster Response Fund (NDRF), as per laid down procedure, in case of disaster of ‘severe nature’, which includes an assessment based on the visit of an Inter-Ministerial Central Team (IMCT).

At the field level, loss assessment under SDRF is done through a joint field survey by Revenue and Agriculture Department officials. Input subsidy becomes payable only where crop loss is assessed at 33% or more. For SDRF, the State conducts its own assessment whereas for NDRF assistance, the State submits a memorandum to the Government of India, after which an IMCT visits and physically verifies the damage.

The loss assessment under SDRF and NDRF is done only during natural calamities in the specific area of the State and is not a regular District level crop loss assessment. Hence, the same cannot be compared with claims settled under PMFBY and RWBCIS.

Following steps have been taken by Govt. of India to for timely settlement of claims under the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana:

Government has undertaken development of National Crop Insurance Portal (NCIP) as a single source of data for subsidy payment, dissemination of information, delivery of services including online enrollment of farmers, uploading/obtaining individual insured famer’s details and transfer of claim amount electronically to the individual farmer’s Bank Account.

In order to rigorously monitor claim disbursal process, a dedicated module namely ‘Digiclaim Module’ has been operationalized for payment of claims from Kharif 2022 onwards. It involves integration of National Crop Insurance Portal (NCIP) with Public Finance Management System (PFMS) and accounting system of Insurance Companies to provide timely & transparent processing of all claims.

W.e.f. Kharif 2024, in case payment is not made timely by Insurance Company, penalty.of 12% is auto-calculated and levied through NCIP.

Delinking of Central Government share of premium subsidy from that of State Governments has been implemented so that farmers can get proportionate claims relating to the Central Government share.

12% penalty is also levied from Kharif 2025 on State Government for delay in release of State share of subsidy.

Opening of ESCROW Account by the State Government concerned for deposit of their premium share in advance as per provisions of the scheme has been made mandatory w.e.f. Kharif 2025 season for bringing financial discipline.

Also, towards leveraging technology in implementation of the scheme, various steps like capturing of yield data/Crop Cutting Experiments (CCEs) data through CCE-Agri App & uploading it on the NCIP, allowing insurance companies to witness the conduct of CCEs, integration of State land records with NCIP and usage of remote sensing-based yield estimation through YES-TECH have already been taken to improve timely settlement of the claims to farmers.

Crop Loss Assessment App (CLAP), developed as a digital mobile application to streamline and improve the efficiency of crop loss assessments for loacalised calamities/post-harvest losses for individual farms and land parcels under PMFBY. The use of CLAP has been made mandatory for all the States/UTs under the scheme.

This information was given by the Union Minister of state for Agriculture and Farmers welfare, SHRI RAMNATH THAKUR in a written reply in the Rajya Sabha.
